title: Can filler move after treatment？
excerpt: So the common question your patients might ask is can filler move so what actually happens with dermal filler is that you inject it into the tissues and it's basically a long chain sugar molecule hyaluronic acid and in the first few minutes two hours it starts to get tangled up w…

segments.text: so the common question your patients might ask is can filler move so what actually happens with dermal filler is that you inject it into the tissues and it's basically a long chain sugar molecule hyaluronic acid and in the first few minutes two hours it starts to get tangled up with the existing molecules in the skin as collagen elastin your gamma aminoglycans basically gets tangled up and it moves much less easily so can it still move yes because i know if i see a patient back with maybe a little lump or something you can often massage that away within two minutes of them returning two weeks later that so that means filler is mobile but i don't find that it's mobile enough that people have to live their lives around it with the exception of the first couple of hours after the treatment you want to be really cautious probably even the first 48 hours with some products that you try your best not to put any pressure on it and that will just help it integrate into the tissues and stay put for longer having said that we do know from

dr master's discovery with ultrasound that some filler will still be present and will be slightly diffused from the place that was originally put even three four seven years after the treatment so the answer is yes it can and actually instead of denying the possibility altogether that it will move it's a good idea that patients know that it's possible that it can shift slightly over time and that's why maybe every two or three years in certain co-auto patients not in everyone you might need to start fresh diffusion is possible it's not a big problem it's not as big as you might think um but it is possible and other places that can make it move will be anywhere where there's lots of movement so if you've done lip treatments occasionally you get a little bump on the oral commissions that's from repetitive mastication or movement and that cause a little bit of collection of product that's another reason why you occasionally get the sense that products are moving is just because the face is moving and you get little focal points of high pressure where the filler seems to drift but that's a much smaller problem than the diffusion over many years i think that's more common so it's possible not a major problem but
your patients should be aware of it and you should be aware of it so that you can look after them in the long term hope that helps

transcript: so the common question your patients might ask is can filler move so what actually happens with dermal filler is that you inject it into the tissues and it's basically a long chain sugar molecule hyaluronic acid and in the first few minutes two hours it starts to get tangled up with the existing molecules in the skin as collagen elastin your gamma aminoglycans basically gets tangled up and it moves much less easily so can it still move yes because i know if i see a patient back with maybe a little lump or something you can often massage that away within two minutes of them returning two weeks later that so that means filler is mobile but i don't find that it's mobile enough that people have to live their lives around it with the exception of the first couple of hours after the treatment you want to be really cautious probably even the first 48 hours with some products that you try your best not to put any pressure on it and that will just help it integrate into the tissues and stay put for longer having said that we do know from dr master's discovery with ultrasound that some filler will still be present and will be slightly diffused from the place that was originally put even three four seven years after the treatment so the answer is yes it can and actually instead of denying the possibility altogether that it will move it's a good idea that patients know that it's possible that it can shift slightly over time and that's why maybe every two or three years in certain co-auto patients not in everyone you might need to start fresh diffusion is possible it's not a big problem it's not as big as you might think um but it is possible and other places that can make it move will be anywhere where there's lots of movement so if you've done lip treatments occasionally you get a little bump on the oral commissions that's from repetitive mastication or movement and that cause a little bit of collection of product that's another reason why you occasionally get the sense that products are moving is just because the face is moving and you get little focal points of high pressure where the filler seems to drift but that's a much smaller problem than the diffusion over many years i think that's more common so it's possible not a major problem but your patients should be aware of it and you should be aware of it so that you can look after them in the long term hope that helps
